abstract: In amine-based post-combustion capture, the prevailing wisdom for managing solvent degradation is the mantra to "keep the solvent clean." This strategy aims to minimize solvent consumption by removing accumulated metals, degradation products, and reactive trace components captured from flue gas—such as $NO_2$—which are believed to drive degradation alongside dissolved $O_2$. However, this hypothesis is largely informed by laboratory experiments using fresh amines and often fails to account for the true complexity of the solvent matrix. Specifically, it overlooks the intricate interactions between potential metal catalysts, degradation products, and oxidizing agents, as well as the specific chemical conditions required for degradation mechanisms to proceed. This study investigates the degradation of the CESAR1 solvent (a 3.0 molar aqueous solution of 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol and piperazine) within this context, challenging the simplistic assumption that cleanliness alone dictates solvent stability.
